title: 1 Corinthians 10
subtitle: Warnings from Israel's History

heb.paragraph: The noun typos (example, pattern) in vv. 6, 11 is a technical term in Pauline typology. In Greek it originally denoted the mark left by a blow (cf. John 20:25, the 'mark' of the nails). Paul extends it to mean a divinely intended pattern: Israel's wilderness experiences are not merely past events but typological prefigurations that speak directly to the eschatological community ('us, on whom the culmination of the ages has come,' v. 11). This is not allegory but salvation-historical correspondence.

The noun peirasmos (testing, temptation) in v. 13 carries a dual sense: external trial and internal enticement. Paul's assurance that God provides a 'way of escape' (ekbasis) with every testing is one of the most quoted pastoral promises in the Pauline corpus. The term ekbasis (literally 'a way out') may allude to the military concept of an escape route from an ambush—God provides not immunity from testing but a viable path through it.

refs.note: The pillar of cloud and fire that guided Israel through the wilderness is the referent of Paul's 'under the cloud' (v. 1). This divine presence prefigures the Spirit's guidance of the church.

The sexual immorality at Baal Peor, where Israelite men 'began to indulge in sexual immorality with Moabite women' who 'invited them to the sacrifices to their gods,' directly parallels the Corinthian situation: idol-food consumption and sexual licence were intertwined in pagan temple practice.

Israel's complaint against God and Moses resulted in the plague of venomous serpents. Paul uses this as a warning against 'testing Christ' (v. 9)—a remarkable christological identification of the pre-incarnate Christ with the God of Israel.

echoes.source_context: Israel passed through the Red Sea with walls of water on either side, the cloud of God's presence leading them. They emerged on the other side as a redeemed community, free from Egypt's power.
echoes.connection: Paul calls this being 'baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea.' The sea crossing was a type of Christian baptism — passage through water into covenant community, leaving the old life behind.
echoes.significance: Paul establishes typological reading as normative: Israel's story is 'for our instruction.' The exodus pattern — deliverance through water by divine power — becomes the template for Christian experience.

echoes.source_context: Moses struck the rock at Horeb and water flowed out for the thirsty people. God provided sustenance in the wilderness through miraculous means — water from stone.
echoes.connection: Paul makes the striking claim: 'the rock was Christ.' The pre-incarnate Christ accompanied Israel as the source of their sustenance. The wilderness rock was christophany.
echoes.significance: This is bold typology: not just that the rock prefigured Christ, but that Christ was actually present with Israel. Paul's Christology extends backward through all of Israel's history.

notes.note: Paul's use of 'our ancestors' (hoi pateres hēmōn) is striking when addressed to a predominantly Gentile church: the Corinthians are incorporated into Israel's story through Christ. The 'spiritual food' and 'spiritual drink' (vv. 3–4) refer to the manna and the water from the rock (Exod 16–17). The identification of the rock with Christ ('that rock was Christ,' v. 4) is a bold typological claim: Christ was present and active in Israel's wilderness experience.

The four wilderness sins—idolatry (v. 7), sexual immorality (v. 8), testing Christ (v. 9), grumbling (v. 10)—form a catalogue that directly mirrors Corinthian problems addressed elsewhere in the letter: idolatry (chs. 8–10), sexual sin (chs. 5–6), testing the Lord's patience (11:27–32), and divisive grumbling (chs. 1–4).

The phrase 'on whom the culmination of the ages has come' (v. 11) establishes the eschatological perspective. The Corinthians live at the climax of redemptive history—not a position of security but of heightened accountability. Verse 13 provides the pastoral reassurance: God's faithfulness sets the limit on every trial and always provides a way through.

Calvin reads Paul's wilderness typology as a direct rebuke to sacramental presumption. Just as Israel enjoyed 'baptism' in the cloud and sea, and 'communion' in the spiritual food and drink, yet most of them perished—so the Corinthians must not assume that participation in Christian sacraments guarantees salvation. The sacraments are means of grace, not magical protections. Calvin emphasises the christological reading of v. 4: the pre-incarnate Christ was already the mediator of Israel's salvation, confirming the unity of the covenant of grace across both Testaments.

Calvin treasures v. 13 as a pillar of pastoral comfort. God's faithfulness means that no believer is ever placed in a situation where sin is the only option. The 'way of escape' is not removal of the trial but the provision of grace sufficient to endure it. Calvin warns against two opposite errors: presumption (thinking one is too strong to fall) and despair (thinking the trial is too great to endure).

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note discusses the Jewish tradition of a 'following rock' (based on the rock appearing at two different locations in Exodus 17 and Numbers 20). Paul may draw on this tradition while giving it a christological interpretation: the 'spiritual rock that accompanied them' was Christ himself—present with Israel throughout the wilderness journey.

The discrepancy between Paul's 'twenty-three thousand' and Numbers 25:9's 'twenty-four thousand' is addressed in the NET note. The most likely explanation is that Paul specifies those who fell 'in one day' (as the text says), while Numbers gives the total count including subsequent judicial executions.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that the four 'all' statements (vv. 1–4: all under the cloud, all through the sea, all ate, all drank) are deliberate and emphatic: universal privilege did not guarantee universal salvation. This directly targets the Corinthian assumption that their spiritual knowledge and sacramental participation render them immune to judgment. Fee notes that the christological identification of the rock with Christ (v. 4) is not allegory but typology grounded in Paul's conviction that the pre-existent Christ was active in Israel's history.

Fee meticulously traces each wilderness sin to its corresponding Corinthian issue: idolatrous feasting (v. 7 = chs. 8–10), sexual immorality (v. 8 = chs. 5–6), testing the Lord (v. 9 = 8:9–12), and grumbling (v. 10 = chs. 1–4). The parallel is not incidental but structurally intentional: Paul has selected precisely those wilderness failures that mirror Corinthian behaviour.

Fee reads v. 13 not as a generic promise of deliverance from trials but as a specific assurance directed at the idol-food crisis: God provides a 'way of escape' from the temptation of idolatrous meals—namely, the option to simply not attend (v. 14: 'flee from idolatry').

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton provides detailed analysis of Paul's typological method, distinguishing it from both allegory (where the historical referent is irrelevant) and mere illustration (where the correspondence is accidental). Pauline typology, Thiselton argues, presupposes a divinely ordered pattern in history: the wilderness generation's experience genuinely prefigures the church's situation because both stand within the same redemptive narrative, now reaching its climactic stage.

Thiselton draws attention to the phrase ta telē tōn aiōnōn ('the ends of the ages,' v. 11), arguing that the plural 'ages' reflects Paul's sense that multiple strands of redemptive history converge in the present eschatological moment. The Corinthians live at the intersection of ages—a position of extraordinary privilege and corresponding responsibility.

hist.context: Paul draws on four specific episodes from the wilderness narrative to warn the Corinthians against spiritual presumption: the golden calf (Exod 32; cited via Exod 32:6 in v. 7), sexual immorality at Baal Peor (Num 25:1–9; v. 8), testing God at Massah/Meribah and via the bronze serpent incident (Num 21:5–6; v. 9), and grumbling in various episodes (Num 14:2, 29, 36–37; Num 16:41–49; v. 10). The number 'twenty-three thousand' in v. 8 (versus Num 25:9's 'twenty-four thousand') has generated extensive discussion; the most common explanation is that Paul cites those who fell 'in one day' while Numbers gives the total including those executed by the judges. The controlling point is unmistakable: sacramental privilege (cloud, sea, spiritual food and drink) does not guarantee divine approval if accompanied by idolatry and immorality.

heb.paragraph: The noun koinōnia (participation, fellowship) is the theological lynchpin of Paul's anti-idolatry argument. At the Lord's Table, believers participate in the body and blood of Christ (v. 16). At pagan sacrificial meals, participants enter into fellowship with demons (v. 20). Paul's argument is not about the metaphysical nature of idols (which are 'nothing,' 8:4) but about the relational reality of cultic participation: sharing a sacrificial meal creates a binding communion with the power behind the altar.

The noun trapeza (table) in v. 21 sets up an exclusive either/or: the 'table of the Lord' versus the 'table of demons.' In the ancient world, a table denoted a sacrificial or communal meal setting. The concept of incompatible tables draws on OT covenant theology (cf. Mal 1:7, 12 LXX, where the altar is called the Lord's trapeza). Paul's point is not that demons possess real divine power but that participating at their 'table' constitutes a betrayal of one's exclusive covenant allegiance to Christ.

refs.note: Paul alludes to Deut 32:17 in v. 20, using similar language about demons behind pagan sacrifice, but this is not a direct citation of the LXX text.

Paul's institution narrative in ch. 11 provides the positive counterpart to this warning: the Lord's Supper is a 'new covenant' meal that proclaims Christ's death until he comes. The two passages together establish that eucharistic participation and idolatrous feasting are irreconcilable.

notes.note: The imperative 'flee' (pheugete) is the same word Paul used for sexual immorality (6:18)—it demands immediate, decisive separation, not cautious negotiation. The Lord's Supper argument (vv. 16–17) establishes the principle: the cup and bread create real participation (koinōnia) in Christ's body and blood. If the Lord's Table creates genuine spiritual communion, then by analogy a pagan sacrificial table creates genuine (though demonic) spiritual communion.

Paul introduces Israel's altar as a middle term: those who eat the sacrifices 'participate in the altar' (v. 18). The analogy is tripartite: Lord's Table = communion with Christ; Israel's altar = communion with God via the covenant; pagan table = communion with demons. The climax (v. 22) invokes divine jealousy—the covenant concept that God will not share His people's allegiance with rivals (cf. Exod 20:5; Deut 4:24).

Calvin reads this passage as the definitive refutation of the Corinthian intellectuals who claim that since idols are 'nothing,' attending pagan meals is harmless. Calvin grants their premise (idols are indeed nothing) but denies their conclusion: behind the idol stands a demonic reality that exploits false worship. The Lord's Supper provides the analogy: just as the bread and cup signify and seal a real participation in Christ, so pagan rites signify and seal a real (though counterfeit) spiritual bond. Calvin notes that the jealousy of God (v. 22) is not an irrational emotion but the righteous demand of the covenant Lord for exclusive loyalty.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note flags the unusual word order: Paul mentions the cup before the bread, reversing the order of the institution narrative (11:23–26). Several explanations have been proposed: (1) the cup is mentioned first because it parallels the 'cup of demons' that is the focus of the argument; (2) Paul follows a liturgical order different from Luke's; (3) the chiastic structure places the cup in the emphatic first position.

The NET note on the citation of Deut 32:17 explains that Paul does not teach that idols are real gods but that demonic powers operate behind idolatrous worship. The sacrifices are offered 'to demons, not to God'—a distinction that renders idol-food consumption spiritually dangerous regardless of the idol's ontological status.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that this passage represents Paul's theological trump card in the idol-food debate. The 'knowledgeable' Corinthians are correct that idols are nothing (8:4), but they have failed to reckon with the demonic reality behind idolatry. Fee contends that Paul's koinōnia argument is not sacramental mysticism but covenant theology: participation at a cultic meal enacts and affirms allegiance to the deity being honoured. The Lord's Table and the demon's table represent incompatible covenant commitments. Fee's Pentecostal background leads him to take the demonic dimension seriously: pagan worship creates genuine exposure to malevolent spiritual forces.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton offers a nuanced analysis of Paul's three-table argument, noting that the term koinōnia functions differently in each case. At the Lord's Table, koinōnia denotes participatory communion with Christ; at Israel's altar, it denotes covenantal identification with God; at the pagan table, it denotes (unwitting) communion with demons. Thiselton argues that Paul's logic is not symmetrical—the Lord's Table creates a positive spiritual bond, while the pagan table creates an objectively harmful one, whether or not the participant intends it. The rhetorical force of the passage depends on the Corinthians accepting the eucharistic premise (which they apparently do) and then recognising its implications for idolatrous meals (which they have not).

hist.context: Paul now moves from historical warning (vv. 1–13) to direct prohibition (vv. 14–22). The command to 'flee from idolatry' (v. 14) is unambiguous and non-negotiable. The argument rests on the analogy between three tables: the Lord's Table (vv. 16–17), Israel's altar (v. 18), and pagan sacrificial tables (vv. 19–21). Participation at each table creates a real communion (koinōnia) with the power being served. Since the Lord's Table and the demon's table represent mutually exclusive allegiances, one cannot partake of both. The rhetorical question 'Are we trying to arouse the Lord's jealousy?' (v. 22) echoes the language of Deuteronomy's covenant-jealousy passages (Deut 32:16–21).

heb.paragraph: The noun syneidēsis (conscience) appears five times in 10:25–29, making it the most concentrated cluster of the term in Paul's writings. In Hellenistic moral philosophy, syneidēsis denoted the faculty of self-evaluation—a retrospective awareness of one's own moral standing. Paul's usage is both broader and more relational: conscience functions not only as self-judgment but as a social-ethical barometer, sensitive to the impact of one's actions on others. The critical distinction in vv. 28–29 is between the believer's own conscience (which is free) and 'the other person's conscience' (which must be respected).

The imperative 'do it all for the glory of God' (v. 31) uses doxa in its distinctively biblical sense: not mere reputation but the weighty manifestation of God's character. The command provides the overarching ethical principle that subsumes all the specific rulings of chs. 8–10. Every decision about food, drink, and social interaction is to be evaluated by a single criterion: does it display God's character to the watching world?

refs.note: Paul's fullest treatment of the conscience principle appears in Romans 14, where the 'strong' must not despise the 'weak' and the 'weak' must not judge the 'strong.' Both passages prioritise love over liberty and communal edification over individual freedom.
The principle that 'whatever you do, whether in word or deed, do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus' parallels the comprehensive ethical vision of 1 Cor 10:31: all of life is worship.

Paul's citation of 'The earth is the Lord's' (v. 26) grounds food freedom in creation theology: since everything belongs to God, no food is inherently defiled. This aligns with Jesus' declaration that all foods are clean (Mark 7:19).

notes.note: Paul quotes the Corinthian slogan—'I have the right to do anything'—for the second time (cf. 6:12) and again qualifies it: not everything is beneficial, not everything is constructive. The criterion shifts from individual freedom to communal edification. Verse 24 states the principle baldly: 'No one should seek their own good, but the good of others.' This other-centred ethic is the hallmark of Christian liberty.

The practical rulings are surprisingly permissive: buy freely in the market; eat whatever is served at dinner. The only restriction is triggered by explicit information that the food was sacrificed to an idol (v. 28). Paul's reasoning is not about ritual contamination but about relational responsibility: eating after being told the food is sacrificial sends a signal of approval that could damage another person's conscience.

The closing summary—'do it all for the glory of God'—establishes the supreme principle that governs all particular rulings. Paul applies it immediately to three social groups: Jews, Greeks, and the church of God (v. 32). The apostle's own practice is to 'please everyone in every way,' seeking not his own good but 'the good of many, so that they may be saved' (v. 33). This verse reprises the missionary accommodation of 9:19–23.

Calvin draws from this passage the foundational Reformed principle of Christian liberty: believers are free in matters not explicitly commanded or prohibited by Scripture, but this freedom is always bounded by the law of love. Calvin warns against two abuses: the legalist who multiplies prohibitions beyond Scripture, and the libertine who exercises freedom without regard for others. The citation of Psalm 24:1 is, for Calvin, a creation-theology anchor: God's sovereign ownership of all things means that no material substance is inherently evil, and the believer may use all creation with thanksgiving.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note explains that the macellum (meat market) was a common feature of Roman cities. Archaeological remains of a macellum have been found at Corinth near the forum. Meat sold there might or might not have come from temple sacrifices, but Paul's instruction is to buy without investigation—a remarkable departure from Jewish kashrut practice.

The NET note addresses the interpretive puzzle of vv. 29b–30: 'Why should my freedom be judged by another's conscience?' Some commentators read this as Paul quoting a Corinthian objection; others (including the NET translators) read it as Paul's own rhetorical question, expressing the frustration of having one's freedom curtailed. The NET favours the latter reading.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that this final section resolves the practical question of idol-food with a nuanced three-tier system: (1) temple meals are absolutely prohibited (vv. 14–22); (2) marketplace meat and private dinners are permitted without inquiry (vv. 25–27); (3) the only restriction is when explicit information about sacrificial origin is provided, at which point the other person's conscience becomes the decisive factor (vv. 28–29). Fee notes that the comprehensive summary of v. 31 ('do it all for the glory of God') prevents the rulings from becoming a new casuistry: the underlying principle is always love-shaped, God-oriented freedom.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton provides socio-rhetorical analysis of the term 'conscience' (syneidēsis) in this passage, noting that its repeated use signals a shift from theological argument (vv. 14–22) to practical pastoral wisdom (vv. 23–33). He argues that Paul distinguishes between conscience as an internal moral faculty and conscience as a social-relational reality: the believer's own conscience is free (v. 29a), but the other person's conscience creates a relational obligation (v. 29b). Thiselton concludes that Paul's ethic is neither individualistic (my rights) nor collectivist (the group decides) but dialogical: freedom is exercised in responsive relationship with others.

hist.context: Paul now addresses the practical middle ground: what about meat purchased in the macellum (meat market, v. 25) or served at a private dinner (v. 27)? Unlike temple banquets (vv. 14–22), these situations do not involve direct cultic participation. Paul's ruling is remarkably liberal: eat freely without investigating the meat's origin ('without raising questions of conscience'). The citation of Psalm 24:1 ('The earth is the Lord's, and everything in it') grounds this freedom theologically: all food belongs to God and is therefore clean. The only exception is when someone explicitly identifies the meat as sacrificial (v. 28)—not because the food changes character but because the informant's conscience and the social signal require restraint.

themes.note: Judgment dominates as Paul warns against presumption via Israel’s wilderness failures. Worship and Covenant score highly given the Lord’s Table / demon’s table contrast and the covenant-jealousy language.

rows.label: vv.1–13
rows.text: Wilderness typology: four sins of Israel as warnings to the Corinthians

rows.label: vv.14–22
rows.text: Three tables: Lord’s Supper, Israel’s altar, pagan sacrifice — incompatible communions

rows.label: vv.23–33
rows.text: Practical rulings: marketplace meat, private dinners, conscience of others

lit.note: Chapter 10 completes the idol-food argument begun in ch. 8. The structure moves from historical warning (Israel) to theological prohibition (temple meals) to practical guidance (marketplace and dinner), with the overarching principle of God’s glory governing all.

hebtext.note: The noun typos (example, pattern) in vv. 6, 11 is a technical term in Pauline typology. In Greek it originally denoted the mark left by a blow (cf. John 20:25, the 'mark' of the nails). Paul extends it to mean a divinely intended pattern: Israel's wilderness experiences are not merely past events but typological prefigurations that speak directly to the eschatological community ('us, on whom the culmination of the ages has come,' v. 11). This is not allegory but salvation-historical correspondence.

Paul's assurance that God provides a 'way of escape' (ekbasis) with every testing is one of the most quoted pastoral promises in the Pauline corpus. The term ekbasis (literally 'a way out') may allude to the military concept of an escape route from an ambush—God provides not immunity from testing but a viable path through it.

The noun koinōnia (participation, fellowship) is the theological lynchpin of Paul's anti-idolatry argument. At the Lord's Table, believers participate in the body and blood of Christ (v. 16). The concept of incompatible tables draws on OT covenant theology (cf. Mal 1:7, 12 LXX, where the altar is called the Lord's trapeza). The noun syneidēsis (conscience) appears five times in 10:25–29, making it the most concentrated cluster of the term in Paul's writings. In Hellenistic moral philosophy, syneidēsis denoted the faculty of self-evaluation—a retrospective awareness of one's own moral standing. The imperative 'do it all for the glory of God' (v. 31) uses doxa in its distinctively biblical sense: not mere reputation but the weighty manifestation of God's character. The command provides the overarching ethical principle that subsumes all the specific rulings of chs. 8–10. Every decision about food, drink, and social interaction is to be evaluated by a single criterion: does it display God's character to the watching world?

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Paul's use of 'our ancestors' (hoi pateres hēmōn) is striking when addressed to a predominantly Gentile church: the Corinthians are incorporated into Israel's story through Christ…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Warnings from Israel's History

translations.text: For I do not want you to be ignorant of the fact, brothers and sisters, that our ancestors were all under the cloud and that they all passed through the sea.
For I do not want you to be unaware, brothers, that our fathers were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea,
We should not test Christ, as some of them did—and were killed by snakes.
We must not put Christ to the test, as some of them did and were destroyed by serpents,

Paul's fullest treatment of the conscience principle appears in Romans 14, where the 'strong' must not despise the 'weak' and the 'weak' must not judge the 'strong.' Both passages prioritise love over liberty and communal edification over individual …

discourse.thesis: Israel's wilderness failures are warnings to the overconfident — participation in the Lord's table is incompatible with participation in idol feasts, for it is the same logic of spiritual communion that makes both dangerous.

nodes.text: Israel's typological warning: all were under the cloud, all passed through the sea, all ate spiritual food, all drank from the spiritual rock that followed them (the Rock was Christ) — nevertheless God was not pleased with most of them. These things happened as examples (τύποι) for us — they craved evil, worshiped idols, committed sexual immorality, tested Christ, grumbled — and were destroyed.

children.text: These things were written for our instruction on whom the end of the ages has come. Let anyone who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall. No temptation has overtaken you that is not common to man — God is faithful and will not let you be tempted beyond your ability, but will provide the way of escape.

nodes.text: Therefore flee from idolatry. The cup of blessing — a participation (κοινωνία) in the blood of Christ; the bread — a participation in the body of Christ. Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body. Consider Israel: those who eat the sacrifices participate in the altar. What do I imply — that idols are something? No. But what pagans sacrifice they offer to demons. You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons.

children.text: The real problem with idol feasts is not the idol (which is nothing) but the demonic power behind it. Participation creates a genuine spiritual bond — not with a non-existent god but with the demonic realm. The logic: communion creates solidarity; two incompatible solidarities cannot coexist.

nodes.text: 'All things are lawful' — but not all things are helpful, not all things build up. Let no one seek his own good but the good of his neighbor. Eat whatever is sold in the market without raising a question. If an unbeliever invites you, eat whatever is set before you. But if someone says 'This has been offered in sacrifice,' do not eat — for the sake of the one who informed you, and for the sake of conscience.

children.text: The governing rule: whatever you do, do all to the glory of God. Give no offense to Jews or Greeks or the church of God — just as Paul tries to please everyone in everything, not seeking his own advantage but that of many, so that they may be saved.

discourse.note: 1 Corinthians 10 completes the idol-food argument (chs. 8-10) with a typological reading of Israel's history and a theology of communion. The argument's logic: the Eucharist creates real participation in Christ; idol feasts create real participation in the demonic. The 'strong' theology (idols are nothing) was correct but incomplete — it missed the participatory dimension of religious eating.

coaching.tip: 'Now these things happened to them as an example, but they were written down for our instruction' (v. 11). Israel's wilderness failures — idolatry, sexual immorality, testing God, grumbling — warn the church. Scripture's history is pedagogy. 'Let anyone who thinks that he stands take heed lest he fall' (v. 12). Overconfidence kills.

'Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God' (v. 31). The idol-meat discussion concludes with a comprehensive principle. Every action — even meals — can glorify God. There's no secular realm. The sacred/secular divide dissolves when glory becomes the aim.

prayer_prompt: As Paul uses Israel's failures as a warning and writes 'if you think you are standing firm, be careful that you don't fall,' consider asking God where overconfidence may be setting you up for a stumble.
